zh.lifeSummary: 當Joan Clark於大約1504年出生在英國肯特萊坦時，她的父親christopher danvers of danby26歲，母親Elizabeth Ferres25歲。她於1525年與Robert Lawrence De Catte在英國英格蘭戴文可利頓聖 安德也斯 邱奇結婚。他們至少是3個兒子和1個女兒的父母。她去世於大約1569年在英國英格蘭肯特希廷波恩享年67歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭肯特希廷波恩。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Joan Clark于大约1504年出生在英国肯特萊坦时，她的父亲christopher danvers of danby26岁，母亲Elizabeth Ferres25岁。她于1525年与Robert Lawrence De Catte在英国英格兰戴文可利頓聖 安德也斯 邱奇结婚。他们至少是3个儿子和1个女儿的父母。她去世于大约1569年在英国英格兰肯特希廷波恩享年67岁，埋葬在英国英格兰肯特希廷波恩。

nameMeanings.name: Clark
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from Middle English clerk, clark ‘clerk, cleric, writer’ (Old French clerc; see Clerc ). The original sense was ‘man in a religious order, cleric, clergyman’. As all writing and secretarial work in medieval Christian Europe was normally done by members of the clergy, the term clerk came to mean ‘scholar, secretary, recorder, or penman’ as well as ‘cleric’. As a surname, it was particularly common for one who had taken only minor holy orders. In medieval Christian Europe, clergy in minor orders were permitted to marry and so found families; thus the surname could become established.
Irish (Westmeath, Mayo): in Ireland the English surname was frequently adopted, partly by translation for Ó Cléirigh; see Cleary .
Americanized form of Dutch   De Klerk   or Flemish   De Clerck   or of variants of these names, and possibly also of French   Clerc  . Compare   Clerk   2 and   De Clark  .

variants.name: Clarke
meanings: English and Irish: variant of   Clark  .

History: This name was brought independently to New England by many bearers from the 17th century onward. Nicholas Clarke was one of the founders of Hartford, CT (coming from Cambridge, MA with Thomas Hooker) in 1635.

variants.name: Clerk
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Jacques.
Americanized form of French   Clerc   and perhaps also of the Dutch and Flemish cognates   De Klerk   and   De Clerck   or of variants of these names. Compare   Clark   3.

variants.name: Clerkin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Liam, Brendan, Dympna, Seamus.

Irish (Monaghan): from an Irish pet form of English clerk ‘cleric’ (Irish cléireach), see Clark . Alternatively, an Anglicized form of Ó Cléireacháin, ‘descendant of little cleric’, an Irish diminutive of the word. See also McClurkin , an Antrim surname from Mac Cléirchín ‘son of little cleric’.

variants.name: Calarco
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Angelo, Carmelo, Fausto, Nunziato, Salvatore.
Italian (southern): probably a nickname derived from Greek kalē archē ‘good beginning’. Compare Clark 4.

variants.name: Clerc
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alain, Odette, Sebastien, Serge.

French: occupational or status name for a member of a minor religious order or for a scholar, Old French clerc, from Late Latin clericus, from Greek klērikos, a derivative of klēros ‘inheritance, legacy’, with reference to the priestly tribe of Levites (see Levy ) ‘whose inheritance was the Lord’. In medieval Christian Europe, clergy in minor orders were permitted to marry and so found families; thus the surname could become established. In the Middle Ages it was virtually only members of religious orders who learned to read and write, so that the term clerc later came to denote any literate man, a scholar (hence its modern meaning ‘scribe, secretary’ or ‘clerk’). Compare Leclerc .

variants.name: Clarkson
meanings: English (Lancashire and Yorkshire): patronymic from   Clark  .

nameMeanings.name: Joan
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Contracted form of Old French Jo(h)anne, from Latin Io(h)anna ( see Joanna ). In England this was the usual feminine form of John from the Middle English period onwards and was extremely popular, but in the 16th and 17th centuries it steadily lost ground to Jane . It was strongly revived in the first part of the 20th century, partly under the influence of George Bernard Shaw's play St Joan ( 1923 ), based on the life of Joan of Arc ( 1412–31 ). Claiming to be guided by the voices of the saints, she persuaded the French dauphin to defy the occupying English forces and have himself crowned, and she led the French army that raised the siege of Orleans in 1429 . The following year she was captured by the Burgundians and sold to the English, and a year later she was burned at the stake for witchcraft at the age of 18 or 19. Her story has captured the imagination of many writers, and she is variously portrayed as a national and political hero, a model of apolitical straightforwardness and honesty, and a religious heroine. She was canonized in 1920 . More recent influences have included the American film actress Joan Crawford ( 1908–77 , born Lucille le Sueur ), the British actress Joan Collins ( b. 1933 ), the American comedienne Joan Rivers ( b. 1933 ), and the West Indian pop singer Joan Armatrading ( b. 1950 ).

variants.name: John
meanings: As a boy's name this represents a revival of Middle English Jan, a by‐form of John , or an adoption of the common European form with this spelling. As a girl's name it is a short form of names such as Janet and Janice .

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

en.lifeSummary: When Joan Clark was born about 1504, in Wrotham, Kent, England, her father, christopher danvers of danby, was 26 and her mother, Elizabeth Ferres, was 25. She married Robert Lawrence De Catte in 1525, in St Andrew's Church, Colyton, Devon, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 3 sons and 1 daughter. She died about 1569, in Sittingbourne, Kent, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 67, and was buried in Sittingbourne, Kent, England, United Kingdom.

ko.lifeSummary: Joan Clark(이)가 대략 1504년 영국 켄트 로섬에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 christopher danvers of danby(은)는 26세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Elizabeth Ferres(은)는 25세였다. 그녀는 1525년 영국 잉글랜드 데폰 코리턴 스트 앤드루'스 c후르치에서 Robert Lawrence De Catte(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 대략 1569년 영국 잉글랜드 켄트 짓틴그보우른에서 67세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 켄트 짓틴그보우른에 묻혔다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Joan Clark родилась около 1504 г. в местности ротам, кэнт, Англия, её отцу по имени christopher danvers of danby было 26 лет, а матери, которую звали Elizabeth Ferres, 25. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени Robert Lawrence De Catte в 1525 г. в местности ст андрю'с чурч, колитон, девон, Англия, Великобритания. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына и 1 дочь. Она умерла около 1569 г. в местности ситтингбурн, кэнт, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 67 лет и была похоронена в местности ситтингбурн, кэнт, Англия, Великобритания.
